title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Witness a Moderate Uptick WoW; Type 1 Transfer Volumes Grow Nearly Fivefold WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(29th September 2025 â€“ 5th October 2025)
  • A total of 1,274,381 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a sharp rise of 376.54% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 496.24% WoW as 3,549,461 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $52.73/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 2.83% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers saw a rise of 2.62%, priced at $56.38/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 5.76% WoW; on 7th October, the spot price stood at $55.50/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $56.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 1.85% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices rose by 2.15% WoW, while D5 price rose by 1.08% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

US Senator, Charles Grassley, led over 45 Senate and House colleagues in urging the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to finalize a rule on imported Renewable Identification Numbers (RINs) and hold firm on the proposed biomass-based diesel volumes. In the letter written to the EPA Administrator: Lee Zeldin, the members of Congress state that such measures would ensure the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) continues to prioritize domestic feedstock, support the interests domestic feedstock and biofuel producers, and create jobs in farming, transportation, and manufacturing. In California, the Governor Newsom signed Assembly Bill, which allows an additional blend of gasoline â€“ E15 fuel specifically â€“ to be sold in the state while the Air Resources Board (CARB) completes its ongoing multimedia evaluation to study whether the additional blend can meet the stateâ€™s clean air requirements.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_605441" align="alignnone" width="758"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 2.83% WoW increase, rising from $51.28 to $52.73 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices rose from $54.94 to $56.38, recording an increase of 2.62%. Type 1 trades witnessed 1,274,381 credits exchanged, recording a 376.54% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 496.24% WoW, reaching 3,549,461 credits. [caption id="attachment_605442" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_605443" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 5.76% WoW, reaching $54.15/MT. On 7th October, the spot price stood at $55.50/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $56.00/MT as of 7th October.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

  [caption id="attachment_605444" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 150 trades were reported in the market, with 26 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $83.52 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 375 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $40.42 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 18 credits. The largest volume of three transfers involved 250,000 credits for Type 2 transactions priced at $51.14, $51.50 and $58.75. The smallest volume of transfer involved 18 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $40.42.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_605445" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $52.86 in October so far, falling from $53.94 in September. Trade volumes for the month of September stood at 2.82M as of 7th October, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_605446" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.Â Across 29th September 2025 â€“ 5th October 2025, total VPT rose by 12.55% WoW, with rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 1.85% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices rose by 2.15% WoW, while D5 price rose by 1.08% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming datesÂ 

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

Grassley Leads Over 45 Colleagues in Calling on EPA to Stand with American Farmers and Biofuel Producers by Finalizing Import RIN Reduction Rule: Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), a lifelong family farmer and a member of the Senate Agriculture Committee, led over 45 Senate and House colleagues in urging the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to finalize a rule on imported Renewable Identification Numbers (RINs) and hold firm on the proposed biomass-based diesel volumes. In their bipartisan, bicameral letter to EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin, the members state such measures would ensure the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) continues to strengthen American energy security and support both American farmers and domestic renewable fuel producers.â€œThe Import RIN reduction also aligns with congressional intent under the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit, which prioritizes domestic feedstocks. Aligning EPA's policy with this standard helps level the playing field for domestic feedstock and biofuel producers with imported feedstocks, such as Brazilian tallow and so called â€˜used cooking oilâ€™ from China, which would otherwise directly undercut U.S. energy, agriculture and manufacturing,â€ the members continued. US Agencies to Continue Some Fossil Fuel Work During Shutdown: Some government staff will be kept on during the federal shutdown to process permits and leases for oil, gas and coal operations on public lands, according to contingency plans published by the Interior Department on Wednesday. Maintaining workers in those areas is aimed at addressing the national energy emergency President Donald Trump declared when he took office in January, the U.S. Bureau of Land Management plan posted by the Interior Department said. "In order to protect human life and federal property and address the National Energy Emergency, BLM staff responsible for processing oil & gas permits/leases, coal energy leases, and other energy and mineral resources necessary for energy production will be excepted or excepted on call, to the extent such plans are necessary to protect human life and federal property," the plan said. Governor Newsom Signs Bill Expanding Fuel Options to Cut Gas Prices: Governor Gavin Newsom signed legislation that could help bring down the cost of gasoline in California. The Governor signed Assembly Bill 30 by Assemblymember David Alvarez (D-San Diego), which allows an additional blend of gasoline â€“ E15 fuel specifically â€“ to be sold in the state while the Air Resources Board (CARB) completes its ongoing work to study whether the additional blend can meet the stateâ€™s clean air requirements. Utility Global and Maas Energy Works Announce Agreement to Develop Dairy Digester-to-Hydrogen Mobility Project in California: Utility Global, Inc., a U.S.-based leader in economical decarbonization technology, and Maas Energy Works, the nationâ€™s leading developer of dairy digester-to-energy projects announced an agreement to advance the development of the first commercial project in California to use dairy digester biogas for direct hydrogen fuel production. The project will produce deeply carbon-negative hydrogen from water without electricity for the mobility sector, setting a new benchmark for cost-effective decarbonization. The project will be located at a dairy farm complex in California and will integrate dairy digester biogas systems with Utilityâ€™s proprietary H2GenÂ® technology to produce economical clean fuel for heavy-duty transportation. This innovative combination will deliver hydrogen with one of the lowest negative carbon intensity scores in the market and provide a scalable pathway to decarbonize heavy-duty transportation economically. The project has also secured initial offtake at attractive pricing to both partners. Under the agreement, MEW will develop, build, and operate the anaerobic digesters and associated biogas treatment systems, while Utility will develop and operate the fuel production system.
